scenario: Rails work session - audit the query Relation contract before a rework

map_quality.rationale: Names Rails as a gem monorepo, places ActiveRecord::Relation exactly at relation.rb:5, lists the relation/ subdir with concrete file names, and gives three entry points (all→default_scoped→relation at named.rb:22, core.rb:442) the agent can start from immediately with only targeted follow-ups.

specificity.rationale: Every abstraction is tied to a file:line: spawn_methods.rb:8, query_methods.rb:9, finder_methods.rb, calculations.rb, delegation.rb, scoping/named.rb:22, core.rb:442 — no vague layer references; the value-constants bag is even cited at relation.rb:54-66.

justification.rationale: States 'lazy query value every finder, scope, and association returns' which is the core WHY, but does not explain WHY the interface is decomposed into focused mixed-in modules (composability, single-responsibility), nor explicitly WHY execution is deferred until enumeration — the answer names the pattern without reasoning about the design choice.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged within the orientation content itself; the single caveat about blast not seeing runtime prepend appears only at the end of the full audit and concerns Step 4+, leaving the agent no guidance on what parts of the orientation to verify before acting.

relationship.rationale: Explicitly states that finders/scopes/associations return a Relation, that the query interface is mixed in from named modules (include list at relation.rb:68-70), and that chaining goes through SpawnMethods#spawn; the all→default_scoped→relation chain is written as a connected path, not a flat file list.

steps.step: Map the Relation contract and what it is built on

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers a complete include list with file:line (relation.rb:68-70), names all five core modules plus Batches/Explain/SignedId/TokenFor/Enumerable, pins the value-bag constants at relation.rb:54-66, and gives the initialize signature at :78 — an agent can immediately know what the contract encompasses. Minor gap: Delegation's delegated-classes mechanism and ClassSpecificRelation are named but their role in the query interface contract isn't mapped as precisely as the query-building modules.

specificity.rationale: Every mixin is tied to a concrete file and line — spawn_methods.rb:8, query_methods.rb:9, finder_methods.rb, calculations.rb, delegation.rb, signed_id.rb:32 — no vague cluster references; the value constants are cited at relation.rb:54-66 with their names, giving the agent exact insertion points.

justification.rationale: The @values-bag explanation ('Constants … define the @values-bag contract every spawn/merge depends on') is the one WHY statement in the step-2 section; the reasoning for why the interface is composed from focused modules, why spawn/merge handles cloning, and why delegation routes to the model class is deferred to step 3 rather than stated here as contract rationale.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged within the step-2 contract section itself — the answer projects confidence on all modules; the single caveat ('can't see runtime prepend/plugin extensions') appears only in the closing note about blast-radius verification, not as a flag on any specific module binding or delegated-method set the agent should verify before relying on.

relationship.rationale: The answer states 'includes' as the binding mechanism and correctly notes that the value-bag constants are what spawn/merge depends on, which is the key structural link; but it does not, within the step-2 section, state what each module specifically supplies — that FinderMethods provides find/first/last, that SpawnMethods clones the relation on chaining, that Delegation routes unknown calls to the model class — leaving the agent to infer those connections rather than read them explicitly.

steps.step: Trace how a query is built up and executed

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full build-and-execute path in numbered order with file:line at every layer: `scoping/named.rb:22` → `query_methods.rb` (@values mutation) → `spawn_methods.rb:9-11` (clone) → `merger.rb:6,17,46` (merge) → `query_methods.rb:1673-1675/:1828` (arel/build_arel) → `relation.rb:1243/:1436/:1460` (to_sql/exec_queries/exec_main_query) → `querying.rb:67` → `database_statements.rb:175`; the agent could follow this path to identify every layer that must be changed.

specificity.rationale: Every method is named exactly — `SpawnMethods#spawn` at `spawn_methods.rb:9-11`, `relation_with` at `:72`, `Relation::Merger/HashMerger` at `merger.rb:6,17,46`, `QueryMethods#arel` at `query_methods.rb:1673-1675`, `build_arel` at `:1828`, `exec_main_query` at `relation.rb:1460`, `_query_by_sql` at `querying.rb:67` — no vague layer names.

justification.rationale: The answer names 'spawn clones the relation' and 'arel memoizes build_arel' but never explains WHY chaining produces a fresh relation (immutability of an unexecuted relation) or WHY execution is deferred until enumeration — the rubric's core questions; it lists the call chain without the causal reasoning.

uncertainty.rationale: The closing note flags that `sense_blast` misses runtime `prepend`/plugin extensions and recommends manual re-verification, but does not specifically flag where the spawn/merge semantics were not fully opened or where the lazy-load path was assumed rather than confirmed.

relationship.rationale: Both the prose (using → arrows) and the numbered JSON list assemble the path as an explicit ordered chain — 'all/scope → QueryMethods setters → SpawnMethods#spawn → merge/merge! → arel/build_arel → to_sql/exec_queries → exec_main_query → _query_by_sql → adapter' — with the connecting role of each step stated, not just the file names.

steps.step: Audit every dependent of the Relation contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer names every area the rubric calls out — interface modules, all seven scattered call sites in activerecord/lib (connection_handling:419, sanitization:214, database_statements:803, integration:164, encryption ExtendedDeterministicQueries:28, signed_id:33, join_dependency:189, named:192, default.rb:132), three subclasses, the ActiveSupport Enumerable cross-gem mirror at :145/:161, and the Railties query_command:165 renderer — grouped into clearly labelled buckets, so the agent has a pick-up-and-work map rather than a re-exploration task.

specificity.rationale: Nearly every dependent carries a concrete file:line pair and a one-phrase role: e.g. 'sanitization.rb:214 Relation === value', 'join_dependency.rb:189,213,183 make_constraints', 'enumerable.rb:145,161 Enumerable#pluck/#pick mirror Calculations'; the handful of interface-module entries (WhereClause, Merger) give line ranges rather than pin-point line numbers but are still actionable.

justification.rationale: The ranked_risk section explains WHY each tier is risky — subclasses break on spawn/init signature change, type-test guards silently stop matching, the encryption prepend depends on method shape of where_values_hash/scope_for_create, the AS mirror diverges if Calculations changes — rather than just listing files; coverage is uneven (a few entries only get a short phrase rather than a because clause) but the critical paths are explained.

uncertainty.rationale: The final paragraph explicitly flags that sense_blast cannot see runtime prepend/plugin extensions and calls out the encryption prepend and third-party gems as requiring manual re-check before merge; the note that 'the bare word is too noisy to grep across the query layer' also acknowledges the search gap that motivated the audit.

relationship.rationale: The answer explicitly categorises each reach mechanism in a dedicated 'case_or_shortname_type_tests' block (=== value, is_a?, when Relation) and names the connecting path for each cross-gem dependent ('mirror Calculations' for AS Enumerable, 'renders relations on the CLI' for railties); the prepend path for encryption ('prepend RelationQueries'), the module-include path for signed_id ('SignedId::RelationMethods'), and the subclass inheritance path are all stated, not just the file endpoints.

steps.step: Trace how relation behavior is reused for associations

map_quality.rationale: The answer gives file:line for every key node: CollectionProxy < Relation at collection_proxy.rb:31, AssociationRelation.create/merge! at association.rb:314-315, AssociationScope#scope at association_scope.rb:21, CollectionAssociation#scope at collection_association.rb:302, and the disable-joins variant. An agent can enter any of these nodes with only targeted reads, not a fresh file walk.

specificity.rationale: All pieces are named with exact file:line (collection_proxy.rb:31, association.rb:314-315/:107, association_scope.rb:21, collection_association.rb:302, scoping/named.rb:36); no vague 'the association code' placeholders.

justification.rationale: The answer names the mechanism ('CollectionProxy < Relation', 'collection behaves as relation') but never explains WHY the association collection is built on Relation — i.e., that the design goal is for association queries to get the same lazy, chainable interface without duplicating finder/calculation logic.

uncertainty.rationale: The closing note flags runtime prepend gaps in general, but the answer does not flag which parts of the association reuse path were not fully opened — for instance, whether CollectionProxy delegates specific methods or whether it relies entirely on inheritance, or whether AssociationScope's constraint building was verified versus inferred.

relationship.rationale: The subclassing path is stated explicitly ('CollectionProxy < Relation') and the constraint-building chain is given (Association#target_scope → AssociationRelation.create.merge! → AssociationScope#scope), but the pieces are presented as a flat list rather than a single written chain (collection association → CollectionProxy subclasses Relation → AssociationScope injects constraints → relation executes), making the connecting route assembable but not explicit.
